concept.headline: Atoms are named constants whose value IS their name. true, false, nil are atoms. :ok and :error are the idiomatic success/failure tags used throughout Elixir.
concept.explanation: An atom like :ok is a constant — its value is the name :ok itself. Atoms are stored as integers internally, making comparison O(1). true, false, and nil are aliases for :true, :false, :nil. Atoms are used as map keys, function return status tags, and module names. The is_atom/1 predicate checks if a value is an atom.
concept.syntax:
# Atoms
:ok
:error
:hello_world

# Boolean atoms
true   # same as :true
false  # same as :false
nil    # same as :nil

# Check
is_atom(:ok)      # true
is_atom(true)     # true
is_nil(nil)       # true

# Atoms are equal to themselves
:ok == :ok        # true
:ok == :error     # false
keyPoints: true, false, nil are atoms (aliases for :true, :false, :nil)
Atoms are compared by identity — extremely fast
:ok / :error — idiomatic success/failure tags in tuple results

demo.intro: Watch atoms being used as status tags and booleans being shown as atom aliases.
demo.code:
status = :ok
IO.puts(status)
IO.puts(is_atom(status))
IO.puts(is_atom(true))
IO.puts(true == :true)
IO.puts(is_nil(nil))
err = :error
IO.puts(err == :ok)
IO.puts(err == :error)

explanation.what: status = :ok — binds the atom :ok to status
explanation.why: Atoms start with : followed by a lowercase name (or quoted for special characters)
explanation.misconception: Atoms are NOT strings — :ok and 'ok' are completely different types. :ok is an atom (constant), 'ok' does not exist in Elixir (single quotes are charlists), and "ok" is a binary string.

explanation.why: IO.puts converts atoms to their string representation (the name without the colon)
explanation.intent: Show how atoms print — without the leading colon
explanation.misconception: IO.puts prints the atom's name without the colon. The colon is just the literal syntax. Use IO.inspect to see :ok printed with the colon for debugging.
is_atom works on any value — it returns true only for atoms, not for strings or integers
In Elixir, true and false are NOT a primitive boolean type — they are atoms :true and :false. This is why is_atom(true) returns true.

explanation.what: true == :true → true — they are the same atom
explanation.intent: Demonstrate that true and :true are identical
explanation.misconception: This equality demonstrates that booleans in Elixir are just conveniently-named atoms. The language could work with only :true and :false — true/false are aliases for readability.

explanation.why: nil (the atom :nil) represents the absence of a value
explanation.intent: Show nil as the absence-of-value atom
explanation.misconception: nil in Elixir is NOT null or null pointer — it is the atom :nil. It has no special memory representation. is_nil(nil) → true; is_nil(false) → false.

explanation.intent: Show :error as the complementary tag to :ok
explanation.misconception: :ok and :error are just conventions — they are not keywords. You could use any atom as a tag, but :ok/:error are universally understood in the Elixir ecosystem.

explanation.why: Two different atoms are never equal
explanation.misconception: Atom comparison is O(1) — they are stored as integers. :ok and :error have different internal integer IDs, so == is a fast integer comparison.

explanation.why: Same atom is always equal to itself
explanation.misconception: Every use of :error in your program refers to the EXACT same atom. There is only one :error atom in the entire runtime — they are interned constants.

prediction.question:
What is the output?

IO.puts(is_atom(false))
IO.puts(false == :false)
prediction.explanation: false is the atom :false — is_atom(false) → true. And false == :false → true (they are the same atom). Output: true then true.

explanation.afterDemo: Atoms are the backbone of Elixir's idioms. The {:ok, value} / {:error, reason} tuple pattern (coming up in Tuples) relies entirely on atoms as discriminators. Pattern matching on atoms lets you write clean, self-documenting code: case result do {:ok, val} -> ... {:error, msg} -> ... end. This pattern replaces exception-based error handling for expected failure cases.
keyTakeaways: :name — atom literal. Value is the name itself.
true, false, nil are atoms — is_atom(true) returns true.
:ok / :error — idiomatic result tags. Used in {:ok, val} / {:error, msg} tuples.

practice.prompt: Create an atom :pending. Print it, then print is_atom(:pending), then check if :pending == :ok.
practice.hint: :pending is an atom. is_atom returns true. :pending != :ok so == returns false.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! :pending prints as 'pending', is_atom → true, :pending != :ok → false.
retryHints: Expected: pending then true then false

summary.title: Atoms & Booleans — Complete
takeaways: :name creates an atom. Value is the name. Comparison is O(1).
true, false, nil are atoms :true, :false, :nil — is_atom(true) returns true.
:ok / :error are idiomatic result tags — foundation of Elixir's error handling pattern.
